briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 18, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Impacts of the Climate Crisis and Social Paradoxes
sections.content: Today's briefing highlights that the climate crisis is not merely an environmental issue but profoundly impacts human health and socioeconomic stability [2, 44]. Amid ongoing calls from experts for the World Health Organization (WHO) to declare climate change an international public health emergency, it has been confirmed that climate change is causing various negative impacts across ecosystems and society as a whole, including disrupting human relationships and threatening famous local peaches in specific regions [43, 51, 53, 69]. Notably, paradoxical situations are emerging both domestically and internationally where infrastructure built to protect residents from climate change exacerbates housing insecurity or displaces residents, indicating that climate adaptation policies must consider social equity [1, 7, 36]. Ecosystem disturbances, such as the early awakening of bees due to warm winters and decreased oxygen levels in rivers, are also continuously reported, warning of the widespread ramifications of the climate crisis [8, 45, 52, 67]. These economic shocks can lead to supply chain disruptions and investment contraction, potentially causing billions of dollars in financial risks related to road and hospital costs [5, 83].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, diverse efforts are actively underway to adapt to the climate crisis and achieve carbon neutrality. Cheongju City is accelerating its climate crisis adaptation measures through 56 projects, strengthening life-oriented initiatives such as expanding heatwave shelters and preventing flooding. Gyeonggi Province has also fully reinforced its heatwave countermeasures, responding immediately when temperatures exceed 38 degrees Celsius [9, 10, 11]. Local governments are actively implementing policies to encourage citizen participation, such as operating experiential promotional booths to spread carbon neutrality practices, expanding resource circulation stores and unmanned collection machines, and running the Chungnam-style Carbon Neutrality Point System [17, 18, 19, 20, 23]. Corporations are also actively signing direct solar PPAs to achieve carbon neutrality goals, and there is a strong movement to support the reduction efforts of greenhouse gas emitting businesses, such as Cheonan City's greenhouse gas reduction consulting support [14, 24, 26, 30]. In the education sector, environmental education is being expanded and daily carbon neutrality practices are being encouraged through initiatives like the Jeonbuk Office of Education's ESG leftover food certification challenge and the reopening of Gangdong-gu Energy Maru facilities [16, 22, 34].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Energy Transition and New Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is accelerating both domestically and internationally, but new challenges are also emerging simultaneously. Domestically, positive policy changes are observed for renewable energy expansion, such as the relaxation of investment limits for local public corporations in renewable energy to 50% of their capital. Companies like Cosmax and Hanwha Q Cells are accelerating their efforts to achieve the 2050 carbon neutrality goal by signing direct solar PPAs [14, 29, 30, 35]. The harmonious development of nuclear power, floating solar, and pumped-hydro storage, collectively known as the 'K-Energy Transition Trio,' is also drawing attention [31]. However, during this transition, blind spots in the management of waste solar panels, waste wind turbines, and Energy Storage System (ESS) waste are being pointed out as problems, raising concerns about the urgent need for a comprehensive, lifecycle management system [21, 32]. Furthermore, the case of Seoul City's renewable energy budget cut reveals uncertainties regarding energy transition behind the policy goal of 'accompanying the vulnerable,' demonstrating the importance of ensuring policy consistency and equity [27].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Change Discussions and Political Landscape
sections.content: In the international community, discussions on climate change are actively unfolding, revealing diverse differences in positions across the political spectrum. The UN General Assembly is set to address a climate change 'obligation' resolution, reflecting the international community's commitment to collective action [40, 77]. The scientific community continues research, such as collecting soil samples to understand climate change, and public awareness is rising, with over 80% of residents in Asian regions like Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore expressing concern about climate change [61, 73]. Meanwhile, former President Donald Trump has expressed skepticism towards climate science, distorting recent revisions to scientific predictions of global warming and cheering the easing of predictions by the UN climate change panel [38, 55, 60, 62, 65, 70, 71]. Such political statements could hinder consensus-building on climate action, and the situation where the United States and Iran are identified as the only major emitters without net-zero targets further highlights the importance of international cooperation [85, 87].

briefing.closing: This concludes today's climate briefing for May 18, 2026. We look forward to seeing you again at the next briefing.

articles.description: [Lee Jae-myung Government's First Year] Climate, Energy, Environment. Kori Nuclear Power Plants Units 1 and 2 in Jangan-eup, Gijang-gun, Busan. Yonhap News. Upon its inauguration, the Lee Jae-myung administration reorganized the Ministry of Environment into the Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Environment, designating climate crisis response and energy transition as national priorities. Since taking office, energy transition has been rapidly pursued in conjunction with economic policies, but there has been criticism of a lack of concrete strategies and implementation in the environmental and ecological sectors.

summary.phenomenon: Upon its inauguration, the Lee Jae-myung administration reorganized the Ministry of Environment into the Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Environment, designating climate crisis response and energy transition as national priorities. The energy transition has been rapidly pursued in conjunction with economic policies.
summary.cause: The government set these as national priorities to address the climate crisis and achieve energy transition.
summary.outlook: Despite the rapid progress in energy transition, there is criticism regarding the lack of concrete strategies and implementation in the environmental and ecological sectors.
